coredata.dc:title: Ionic liquid-capped graphene quantum dots as label-free fluorescent probe for direct detection of ferricyanide
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Xue
Qian, Yuting
Jiao, Yajie
Liu, Jiyang
Xi, Fengna
Dong, Xiaoping
coredata.dc:description: Despite complex molecular and atomic doping, efficient post-functionalization strategies for graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are of key importance to control the physicochemical properties and broaden the practical applications. With ionic liquid as specific modification agents, herein, the preparation of ionic liquid-capped GQDs (IL-GQDs) and its application as label-free fluorescent probe for direct detection of anion were reported. Hydroxyl-functionalized GQDs that could be easily gram-scale synthesized and possessed single-crystalline were chosen as the model GQDs. Also, the most commonly used ionic liquids, water-soluble 1-butyl-3-methyl imidazolium tetrafluoroborate (BMIMBF4) was chosen as the model IL. Under the ultrasonic treatment, BMIMBF4 easily composited with GQDs to form IL-GQDs. The synthesized IL-GQDs were characterized by atomic force microscopy (AFM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and fluorescence (FL) spectrum. After successful combination with IL, the excitation-independent photoluminescence behavior of GQDs presented almost no change, whereas, the anion responsiveness of IL-GQDs drastically improved, which afforded the IL-GQDs a sensitive response to Fe(CN)6 3−. Based on the strong fluorescence quench, a facile and sensitive detection of Fe(CN)6 3− was achieved. A wide linear range of 1.0×10−7 to 2.5×10−3 moll−1 with a low detection limit of 40 nmol l−1 was obtained. As the composition and properties of IL and GQDs could be easily tuned by varying the structure, ionic liquids-capped GQDs might present promising potential for their applications in sensing and catalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ionic liquid-capped GQDs
Composite
Fluorescent
Direct detection of anion
Fe(CN)6 3−

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent detection of Fe(CN)6 3− using IL-GQDs
Photoluminescent characteristics of the GQDs and IL-GQDs
Selective FL response of IL-GQDs towards anion
Direct detection of Fe(CN)6 3− using IL-GQDs as FL probe
Quantitation detection of Fe(CN)6 3- in real water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FL emission spectra of GQDs and IL-GQDs excited at 470nm. (b) FL emission spectra of IL-GQDs obtained with excitation wavelength progressively being increased from 400 to 470nm (wavelength interval between adjacent lines was 10nm). The insets are the photographs of GQDs (left 1 and 3) and IL-GQDs (left 2 and 4) aqueous solution under visible light and UV light of 365nm, respectively.

(a) Full-scan XPS, (B) high-resolution C 1s, (C) high-resolution O 1s spectra of the GQDs. (d) Full-scan XPS, (e) high-resolution C 1s, (f) high-resolution O 1s, (g) high-resolution N 1s, (h) high-resolution B 1s, (i) high-resolution F 1s spectra of the IL-GQDs.

(a, b) Low-magnification TEM and (c, d) HRTEM images of the as-prepared IL-GQDs. The inset in Fig. c was the corresponding size distribution of the IL-GQDs. The labeled number in figure d was the lattice spacing of IL-GQDs.
AFM images of the GQDs (a) and IL-GQDs (b) on mica substrate. Inset in each figure was the height profiles along the lines.

(a) The variation of (F0-F)/F0 of IL-GQDs and GQDs in presence of different anions (excitation at 470nm). The concentration of all anions was set as 1.0×10−3 moll−1. (b) Variation of (F0-F)/F0 of IL-GQDs and GQDs in presence of different cautions (excitation at 470nm). The concentration of all cations was set as 1.0×10−3 mol l−1. AA was used as the masking agent and its concentration was set as 2.5×10−3 moll−1.

(a) Fluorescent spectra of IL-GQDs in the presence of different concentrations of Fe(CN)6 3−. Curves of a–m (from top to bottom) represented the concentrations of Fe(CN)6 3− as 0, 1.0×10−7, 1.0×10−6, 5.0×10−6, 1.0×10−5, 5.0×10−5, 1.0×10−4, 2.5×10−4, 5.0×10−4, 1.0×10−3, 1.5×10−3, 2.0×10−3, 2.5×10−3 mol l−1, respectively. Insets were photos of IL-GQDs under daylight and 365nm UV light (from left to right) containing Fe(CN)6 3−(1.0×10−3 moll−1). (b) Variation of (F0-F)/F0 as a function of the concentration of Fe(CN)6 3−.
Schematic illustration for the preparation of green IL-GQDs and turn-off detection of Fe(CN)6 3−.
Detection of Fe(CN)6 3− in water samples. a .

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Reaction conditions: IL-GQDs (pH=7.0, tris-HCl buffer solution, 0.02mg/mL), reactions time: 30min, excitation wavelengths: 470nm.

head.ce:title: Ionic liquid-capped graphene quantum dots as label-free fluorescent probe for direct detection of ferricyanide

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Xue Sun and Yuting Qian contributed equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Despite complex molecular and atomic doping, efficient post-functionalization strategies for graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are of key importance to control the physicochemical properties and broaden the practical applications. With ionic liquid as specific modification agents, herein, the preparation of ionic liquid-capped GQDs (IL-GQDs) and its application as label-free fluorescent probe for direct detection of anion were reported. Hydroxyl-functionalized GQDs that could be easily gram-scale synthesized and possessed single-crystalline were chosen as the model GQDs. Also, the most commonly used ionic liquids, water-soluble 1-butyl-3-methyl imidazolium tetrafluoroborate (BMIMBF4) was chosen as the model IL. Under the ultrasonic treatment, BMIMBF4 easily composited with GQDs to form IL-GQDs. The synthesized IL-GQDs were characterized by atomic force microscopy (AFM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and fluorescence (FL) spectrum. After successful combination with IL, the excitation-independent photoluminescence behavior of GQDs presented almost no change, whereas, the anion responsiveness of IL-GQDs drastically improved, which afforded the IL-GQDs a sensitive response to Fe(CN)6 3−. Based on the strong fluorescence quench, a facile and sensitive detection of Fe(CN)6 3− was achieved. A wide linear range of 1.0×10−7 to 2.5×10−3 moll−1 with a low detection limit of 40 nmol l−1 was obtained. As the composition and properties of IL and GQDs could be easily tuned by varying the structure, ionic liquids-capped GQDs might present promising potential for their applications in sensing and catalysis.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new class of luminescent carbon nanomaterials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have rapidly attracted great research interests as intriguing candidates for photoluminescent (PL) materials [1–6]. Compared with conventional semiconductor quantum dots (e.g. CdS, CdSe, CdTe and PbS) and rare elements, GQDs are more biocompatible and environmentally friendly. In addition, the stability of photoemission is higher than organic fluorescent dyes and the cost is lower than noble metal (e.g. Au, Ag) clusters. As great efforts have been paid on developing new preparing strategies, the synthetic methods gradually mature at present and a large number of GQDs have been successfully obtained through both “top-down” and “bottom-up” methodologies [7–13]. For example, Wang et al. reported the gram-scale synthesis of single-crystalline GQDs by a facile molecular fusion route under mild and green hydrothermal conditions [14]. Though GQDs have exhibited significant potential in biology, optical devices and sensing applications, their potential for a spectrum of applications are still ongoing. For instance, the sensing platform for cations has been widely reported, the application for direct detection of anions is nevertheless still rare (only few indirect detection using on-off strategy have been reported [15–17]). Consequently, further extending the arsenal of GQDs materials is still of critical significance. In spite of complex molecular and atomic doping, the efficient post-functionalization is also extremely important because it provides a convenient route to control the physicochemical properties of GQDs and therefore broadens their practical applications.

Ionic liquids (ILs) are composed of organic cation and inorganic or organic anion. ILs are regarded as one of the most exciting scientific discoveries in chemical science because of their unique properties such as negligible vapor pressure, thermal stability, recyclability, ionic conductivity, the ability to dissolve a wide range of chemical species, and easy of structural design [18,19]. Moreover, ILs possessed excellent anion exchange properties. Due to those unique properties, ILs-capped carbon nanomaterials have represented an interesting class of materials owing to their exceptional properties arising from the synergistic combination of both components. Due to π-π or cation-π interactions, ILs could easily combine with carbon materials to form composites. For example, ILs-capped carbon nanotubes (CNTs), carbon nanofiber and graphene have been extensively reported and applied as matrix with excellent solubility and conductivity for electroanalysis [19]. In case of PL carbon materials, ILs-capped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) also demonstrated unique properties. For instance, Wang et al. used citric acid monohydrate as a carbon source and 1-aminopropyl-3-methyl-imida-zolium bromide (APMImBr) ionic liquid as both modification agent and reaction medium to synthesize APMImBr-capped CQDs [20]. With the modification of ILs, [APMIm][Br]-capped CQDs showed high thermal stability and anion responsiveness i.e., CQDs spontaneously transferred from aqueous phase to ethyl acetate phase once the anion of N(CF3SO2)2 - was introduced. Wang et al. also prepared highly charged CQDs through one-pot pyrolysis with citric acid as carbon source and APMImBr as capping agent. Due to the IL modification, the as-prepared CQDs depicted a high quantum yield (25.1%) and its amphiphilicity can be facilely tuned by anion exchange, which resulted in spontaneous phase transfer between water and oil phase [21]. Liu et al. applied the microwave-hydrothermal treatment for rice straw in the mixed solvent of water and ionic liquid (1-allyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride, AMIMCl). AMIMCl helped to dissolve the cellulose in straw and provided nitrogen atoms for the resulting heteroatom doped CQDs. Due to the surface passivation or functionalization of IL, the produced CQDs possessed high quantum yield (22.58%), which was higher than those of the other prepared from biomass. In addition, the CQDs can serve as an effective label-free fluorescent sensor for the detection of Fe(III) ions with a very low detection limit (200nmol l−1) [22]. To the best of our knowledge, the preparation of ionic liquid-capped GQDs composite with anion responsiveness and their application for direct sensing of anion were few.

With IL as specific modification agents, in this work, we reported the preparation of IL-capped GQDs and its application as label-free fluorescent probe for direct detection of anion. hydroxyl-functionalized GQDs that possessed single-crystalline and could be easily gram-scale prepared were chosen as the model GQDs. Also, the most commonly used and water soluble ionic liquid, BMIMBF4 was chosen as the model IL. With the ultrasonic treatment, BMIMBF4 conveniently was combined with GQDs to form IL-GQDs ( Scheme 1). The role of ILs might lies in two aspects. Firstly, IL could easily combine with GQDs to form composites due to π-π or cation-π interactions. Secondly, IL possessed excellent anion exchange properties. Consequently, more interaction or shorter action distance might occur between IL-GQDs and specific anion, resulting in improved fluorescence quenching and analytical performance. In comparison with GQDs, IL-GQDs presented an almost unchanged PL behavior and the evidently promoted anion responsiveness. Its fluorescence could be strongly quenched in the presence of Fe(CN)6 3−. On this basis, a facile and sensitive detection of Fe(CN)6 3− was achieved. The preparation strategy and the main characteristics of IL-GQDs were described and discussed in detail.

Ionic liquid, 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate (BMIMBF4), , and tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (tris) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (China). Pyrene (purity >97%) was obtained from Chengdu Cologne chemical reagent company (China). The other chemicals used in this work were obtained from Hangzhou Gaojing Chemistry Co. Ltd. (China). All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (18.2MΩcm, Milli-Q, Millipore). Solutions containing different cation and anion were applied. The solution of Ag+ was prepared from its nitrate salts. The solution of Pb2+ was prepared from its acetic acid salt. The solutions of Fe3+, Al3+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, K+ and Na+ were prepared from their chloride salts. To avoid the hydrolysis of metal ions, pH in the preparation and the following experiments was controlled. Briefly, pH values for Fe3+, Hg2+, Cr3+, and Al3+ were controlled at 2.2, 2.5, 2.5 and 3.0 using glycine-HCl buffer, respectively. Cu2+(pH 5.0), Zn2+(pH 6.5), Na+(pH 7.0), Pb2+(pH 7.0), K+(pH 7.0), Cd2+(pH 7.0), Mg2+(pH 7.0), Ca2+(pH 7.0) solutions were prepared in tris-HCl buffer solution. For Ag+, pH value was set as 6.5 adjusted using Tris or HNO3. For the used anions, solutions of NO3 -, S2-, NO2 -, S2O3 2-, HCO3 -, H2PO4 -, HPO4 2-, CH3COO- and Cl- were prepared from their sodium salts. The solutions of Br-, Cr2O7 2-, Fe(CN)6 3-, Fe(CN)6 4-, S2O8 2- and SCN- were prepared from their potassium salts. The pH of anion solution in the preparation and use was controlled by adjusting with tris or HCl. Briefly, pH values for NO3 -, NO2 -, Cl-, Br-, Fe(CN)6 3- and Fe(CN)6 4- solutions were set as 7.0. The pH for solutions of S2O3 2-, HPO4 2-, and CH3COO- was controlled as 9.0. The pH for solutions of S2O8 2-, Cr2O7 2-, H2PO4 -, and SCN- was controlled as 3.5, 4.0, and 4.5, respectively. The solution of S2- solutions were prepared in NaHCO3-Na2CO3 buffer solution at pH 11.5.

The fluorescence (FL) spectrum and intensity were recorded on an RF-5301PC spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) photograph was taken on a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope at operating voltage of 200kV (JEOL Ltd., Japan). The sample was dispersed in water and then drop-casted on ultrathin carbon-coated copper grid. For atomic force microscopy (AFM) characterization, the aqueous solution of GQDs or IL-GQDs was spotted onto freshly cleaved mica surface and dried in air. The samples were measured in air by tapping mode on MFP-3D AFM microscope (Asylum research). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterizations were conducted by using a PHI5300 electron spectrometer using 250W, 14kV, Mg Kα radiation (PE Ltd., USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the IL, GQDs and IL-GQDs were obtained using a Spectrum 5700 (Nicolet Instrument Co. USA).
IL-GQDs were prepared by combination of IL with pre-synthesized GQDs (Scheme 1).

Firstly, GQDs with hydroxyl groups were prepared according to the literature [14] with little modification. The synthesis involved the hydrothermal treatment of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene in alkaline NaOH solution for molecular fusion. Briefly, 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (2mg/mL) was dispersed in NaOH solution (150mL, 5mg/mL) by ultrasonically treating for 1h. The obtained mixture was transferred to poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave and heated at 200°C for 4h. After cooling down to room temperature, the resulting GQDs solution was filtered through a 0.45µm microporous membrane to remove undissolved carbon product, and dialysed by dialysis bag (3500Da) against water for 3 days to remove the molecular precursors. The obtained solution in dialysis bag (GQDs >3500Da) was further dialysed with retained molecular weight of 6000Da for 3 days to remove impurities with large size. Finally, the obtained GQDs (3500–6000Da) were freeze dried to a brown powder.

Secondly, IL-GQDs were prepared by mixing GQDs solution (3500–6000Da, 0.02mg/mL) with BMIMBF4 (100:1, V/V) and ultrasonically treating for 30min. The formed solution was dialyzed by dialysis bag (1000Da) against water for 1 day to remove unbound IL.

Quantum yield (QY) of the GQDs and IL-GQDs was measured by using Rhodamine B as the standard sample and was computed according to the following equation:
Y μ = Ys ×
F
u
A
s
F
s
A
μ
where Y
μ and Y
s are the quantum yield of the GQDs or IL-GQDs and the standard substance, F
u and F
s respectively stand for the integral fluorescence intensity of the test GQDs or IL-GQDs and the diluted standard solution and A
μ and A
s are the maximum absorbance value of GQDs or IL-GQDs and the diluted standard solution, respectively [22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of Fe(CN)6 3− using IL-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: A solution of IL-GQDs (0.02mg/mL) in 50mM tris-HCl buffer solution (pH=7.0) was used as an FL probe toward Fe(CN)6 3−. In a typical run, IL-GQDs solution was mixed with different amount of Fe(CN)6 3−. After the resulting solution was shaken well and incubated for 30min at room temperature, the FL emission spectra (excited at 470nm) were recorded. The detection measurements were performed in triplicate. The relative FL intensity (F0- F)/F0 versus Fe(CN)6 3− concentration were used for calibration. Here, F0 and F are the FL intensities of IL-GQDs in the absence and presence of different concentration of Fe(CN)6 3−, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescent characteristics of the GQDs and IL-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The PL characteristics of both GQDs and IL-GQDs were investigated to evaluate the effect of IL. Results were given in Fig. 1. As illustrated in the inset of the figure, both GQDs (pH=7.0, 0.02mg/mL) and IL-GQDs (pH=7.0, 0.02mg/mL calculated using GQDs) were pale-yellow, transparent solution under daylight, indicating their hydrophilic characters. Such property might be ascribed to the large amount of hydroxyl groups in GQDs and the hydrophilicity of the water-soluble ionic liquid, BMIMBF4. When the aqueous suspensions of GQDs and IL-GQDs were UV irradiated (365nm), strong green fluorescence without obvious difference was observed. To further study the FL property, FL spectra were recorded. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the spectra of GQDs in the absence and presence of ionic liquids were almost constant when excited at 470nm. In addition, the emission spectra of GQDs and IL-GQDs with progressively increased excitation wavelengths from 400 to 470nm with 10nm increment were recorded. Excitation-independent PL behaviors were revealed, suggesting single-emission fluorescence center and high crystallinity [14]. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of the GQDs and IL-GQDs were revealed at 470nm and 512nm, respectively (Fig. 1 b, Fig. S1). The above results confirmed that the incorporation of IL did not change the FL characteristics of GQDs including the maximum emission wavelength and intensity. Also, significant improvement of the FL intensity was not observed in this IL-GQDs composite.

The FL intensity of the IL-GQDs at different pH was studied. The pH of the solution was adjusted by NaOH or HCl (Fig. S2). FL intensity was low at such strong acidic conditions, whereas, it increased dramatically with the increase of pH value. When pH was above 6, fluorescent intensity was relative stable. The phenomenon might be ascribed to the ionization of hydroxyl groups of GQDs [14]. It is well known that electronic structure and charge density might affect the optical characteristics of GQDs. The QYs of GQDs and IL-GQDs were 12.1%，11.8% at pH 7.0, respectively. Photo bleaching experiment of IL-GQDs was also performed to evaluate the photo stability. IL-GQDs were irradiated continually for 4h under UV lamp (365nm). Results were shown in Fig. S3. It was demonstrated that the FL intensity of IL-GQDs can maintain 94% of the original signal with four-hour irradiating under UV light, indicating good photo stability.

Elemental analysis of the GQDs and IL-GQDs was performed by XPS. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the full-scan XPS spectrum confirmed that GQDs were composed of carbon and oxygen. Fine-structure spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 2 a) exhibited two main peaks 286.6eV and 284.6eV, implying the C˭C and C-OH groups due to the graphitic and hydroxyl groups modified carbon atoms (Fig. 2 a). The only one peak for O 1s was ascribed to hydroxyl groups formed from the molecular fusion of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene in alkaline NaOH solution under the hydrothermal treatment (Fig. 2 a). Those results were consistent with the results reported in the literature [14]. As known, 1,3,6-trinitropyrene possessed the same mother nucleus structure with graphene and could occur molecular fusion in hydrothermal reaction to form graphene framework [14]. As the powerful electrophilicity of nitro groups, the addition reaction can happen with electron-rich -OH groups and resulted in GQDs with a large amount of hydroxyl groups. In the case of IL-GQDs, the full range XPS spectrum demonstrated the presence of C, N, O, B and F (Fig. 2 d). The corresponding C1s, N1s, O1s, B1s and F1s peaks centered at 284eV, 403eV, 534eV, 194.6eV and 684.5eV, respectively. Despite C˭C or C-C (284.6eV) and C-OH (286.8eV), C-N centered at 285.9eV was observed corresponding to the presence of N element in IL. Similarly with O 1s of GQDs, only OH groups were observed. The N1s peaks at 401.8eV and 403.6eV are ascribed to the imidazole ring and C-N, the two kinds of in amide group and imidazole ring of BMIMBF4. The presence of bromine and fluorine further proved the successful attachment of IL onto GQDs. The interaction between IL and GQDs might lie in π-π or cation-π interactions and a carbon core with peripheral cationic-ionic pairs might form. FTIR spectrum was also used to confirm the groups of GQDs and IL-GQDs (Fig. S4). Both the GQDs and IL-GODs showed absorption of hydroxyl group (3400cm−1) and C-OH (1150cm−1). Compared with GQDs, the FT-IR spectrum of IL, IL-GQD showed the additional absorption peak from C-N stretching vibration at 1169cm−1. The peak around 1572 and 1465cm−1 in IL and IL-GQDs were related to the typical stretching of imidazole ring [23]. These results were consistent with the results from XPS and further confirmed the successful hybridization of IL with GQDs.

The morphology characteristics of IL-GQDs were investigated using TEM and AFM. TEM images revealed that IL-GQDs were well-dispersed ( Fig. 3 a, b) with relatively uniform size distribution of 1.6–2.4nm. The average diameter of the IL-GQDs was calculated to be 2.0nm (inset of Fig. 3 c). High resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 3 d) showed clear lattice structure of the IL-GQDs, indicating high crystallinity. The lattice spacing was measured to be about 0.23nm, which can be attributed to the (100) facets of graphite [14]. AFM was used to evaluate the height profiles of GQDs and IL-GQDs. As demonstrated in Fig. 4, GQDs showed a typical topographic height of about 2.8nm, indicating that most of the GQDs consisted of 3–5 layer of graphite. For IL-GQDs, the topographic height increased to about 3.4nm, confirming the modification of IL onto GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective FL response of IL-GQDs towards anion
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs-based FL sensing system has been widely reported. The selectivity of cations was mainly achieved based on coordination or electrostatic effect because GQDs usually contained carboxyl or hydroxyl groups or doped hetero atoms (e.g. N, S). However, research on direct anion responsiveness of GQDs is nevertheless still rare. Due to the unique ion-exchange property of IL, the IL-GQDs in this work were expected to possess anion responsiveness.

To test the selectivity of anion, the FL quenching experiments of both GQDs and IL-GQDs were investigated. The FL quenching efficiency of (F0-F)/F0 was determined. Here, F0 and F were the FL intensities of GQDs or IL-GQDs in the absence and presence of different ion, respectively. The commonly used anions including HCO3 -, H2PO4 -, S2O8 2-, SCN-, HPO4 2-, Ac-, Cl-, NO3 -, S2-, NO2 -, Br-, S2O3 2-, Cr2O7 2-, Fe(CN)6 3- and Fe(CN)6 4− were tested. As given in Fig. 5 a, the FL of GQDs could be slightly quenched by Cr2O7 2-, Fe(CN)6 3- and Fe(CN)6 4− with FL quenching efficiency between 0.1 and 0.2. The other anions only caused faintly FL quenching with quenching efficiency below 0.1. In case of IL-GQDs, the same anion selectivity was revealed, whereas, the fluorescence quenching efficiency greatly improved towards Fe(CN)6 3- and Fe(CN)6 4−. For Fe(CN)6 3-, the highest fluorescence quenching efficiency was observed. In order to eliminate possible cation interference, the selectivity towards the commonly used cations was also determined including Fe3+, Cr3+, Na+, Pb2+, K+, Cu2+, Cd3+, Al3+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ag+, Hg2+. Among the tested ions, only Fe3+ possessed remarkable quenching for the FL of IL-GQDs (Fig. 5 b). That may interfere with the detection of Fe(CN)6 3−. This issue can be circumvented by using ascorbic acid (AA) as chelating agents for Fe3+ ions [24]. The above results showed that Fe(CN)6 3− present the highest quenching for the FL of IL-GQDs over the other anions and cations. In comparison with GQDs, also, the IL-GQDs as a FL probe promised much improved selectivity for Fe(CN)6 3−. The reasons might lie in three aspects. Firstly, the used GQDs might have selectivity towards iron based ions. Secondly, the corporation of IL on GQDs might regulate the physicochemical property, especially the electrostatic property, of the GQDs. Compared with GQDs, IL-GQDs might present a carbon core with peripheral cationic-ionic pairs. Thus, the electrostatic effect between Fe(CN)6 3− and IL-GQDs might be promoted. Thirdly, Fe(CN)6 3− was bulky anion and could anion-exchange with BF4 - of IL (Scheme 1). More systematic work on the mechanism of such selectivity was under way in our laboratory. By controlling the redox property of the medium solution, Fe(CN)6 3− and Fe(CN)6 4− can be easily distinguished. For example, Fe(CN)6 3− will transfer to be Fe(CN)6 4− in presence of hydroxylamine hydrochloride. On the other hand, Fe(CN)6 4− will be converted into Fe(CN)6 3− with oxidizing agent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Direct detection of Fe(CN)6 3− using IL-GQDs as FL probe
ce:section.ce:para: FL quenching of IL-GQDs caused by Fe(CN)6 3− at different pH was further explored. To adjusted the pH of the solution, glycine-HCl buffer solution (pH=3), tris-HCl buffer solution (pH 4–9) and carbonate buffer solution (pH 10–11) were used. The concentration of the buffer solution was set as 30mmoll−1. As the buffer solution introduced anions and cations, the effect of the buffer solution was firstly studied. The fluorescence intensities of IL-GQDs in buffer solution were compared with those obtained by adjusting pH only with NaOH or HCl. Consistent results with change no more than 5% at maximum emission wavelength were obtained (Fig. S5), indicating no significant effect caused by the introduced by anions and cations in the buffer solutions. Then the fluorescence quenching caused by Fe(CN)6 3− was studied by measuring the fluorescence intensity in absence (F0) and presence (F) of Fe(CN)6 3−(Fig. S5) and calculating the relative change as (F0-F)/F0 (Fig. S6). Obviously, the values of (F0-F)/F0 had great changes at pH 3.0–6.0 (Fig. S6). As most of the environmental or biological water bodies are neutral or nearly neutral, the neutral solution (pH 7.0) that gave the highest FL quenching ratio was chosen for further investigation.

The kinetic behavior of reactions between the IL-GQDs and Fe(CN)6 3− was investigated by plotting relative FL intensities (F/F0) as a function of time. The reactions reached the quenching equilibrium in about 30min (Fig. S7). Therefore, 30min was chosen as reaction equilibrium time in the subsequent detection experiments. As Fe(CN)6 3− presented the highest FL quenching efficiency for the IL-GQDs over the other anions and cations, a quantitative analysis of Fe(CN)6 3− based on direct FL quenching of IL-GQDs was performed. IL-GQDs solution reacted with different concentrations of Fe(CN)6 3− and the FL spectra were measured. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the FL intensity of IL-GQDs decreased with increase of the concentration of Fe(CN)6 3−, proving the validity of the FL assay for detection of Fe(CN)6 3−. The dependence of (F0-F)/F0 on the concentration of Fe(CN)6 3− ions was concluded in Fig. 6 b. Linear correlation existed between the value of (F0-F)/F0 at 512nm and the concentration of Fe(CN)6 3− in the range of 1.0×10−7 to 2.5×10−3 moll−1. A well-resolved linear part (R2=0.994) was found in the concentration range of 1.0×10−7-−5.0×10−5 mol l−1 and another linear part (R2=0.995) presented in the concentration range of 5.0×10−5−2.5×10−3 moll−1. The detection limit was calculated to be 40 nmol l−1 [25]. The direct determination strategy was different form the indirect detection of anion that reported in the literature. For example, Zhao et al. fabricated CQDs containing plenty of carboxylate moieties [15]. After coordinated with Eu3+ ions, the fluorescence of CQDs turned off. As Eu3+ displayed higher affinity with oxygen-donor atoms from phosphate (pi) than that with the ones from the carboxylate groups of CQDs, turn-on FL can be observed with the addition of pi due to the strong coordination between pi and Eu3+. Thus, the indirect determination of anion was achieved. Obviously, the direct detection present in this work was much more convenient.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitation detection of Fe(CN)6 3- in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of IL-GQDs for detecting Fe(CN)6 3− in real river water was further evaluated. Determination of Fe(CN)6 3− in the water from Jinsha Lake in Hangzhou (China) was then carried out. Before use, the river samples were filtered through a membrane (0.22µm) and then centrifuged (15000 r/min) for 10min. When Fe(CN)6 3−(0.015–1.5mM) was added to such river sample, the recoveries of three samples were between 91.3% and 95.3% ( Table 1). The detection measurements were performed in triplicate and the RSD was below 2.0%. The results indicated that this FL probe is promising for direct Fe(CN)6 3- detection in real samples.

With ionic liquid as specific modification agents, herein, the preparation of ionic liquid-capped GQDs (IL-GQDs) and its application as label-free FL probe for direct detection of anion were reported. The advantages of the proposed method and the prepared IL-GQDs lie in four aspects. (1) After successful combination with IL, the IL-GQDs did not only preserve the excitation-independent PL behavior of GQDs, but also presented the markedly enhanced anion responsiveness, which afforded the IL-GQDs a sensitive response to anion. (2) Based on the improved FL quenching, a facile and direct FL sensor for sensitive detection of Fe(CN)6 3− was achieved with wide linear range and low detection limit. (3) Compared with complex molecular and atomic doping, the modification of GQDs with functional ILs provided a simple and efficient post-functionalization way to adjust the physicochemical properties of GQDs. (4) As the composition and properties of IL and GQs could easily be tuned by varying the structure, ionic liquids-capped GQDs might present promising potential for broadening the practical applications of GQDs in sensing and catalysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21305127), the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LY15B050006, LY17B050007), the Science Foundation of Zhejiang Sci-Tech University (13062173-Y) and 521 talent project of ZSTU.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2016.12.085.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
